title: Stifado
subtitle: Greek slow-braised beef with whole shallots, red wine, vinegar, and warm spices.
excerpt: A long slow Greek stew. Beef chuck and whole baby shallots braise together for two and a half hours in red wine, wine vinegar, tomato, cinnamon, and cloves. The shallots stay whole and turn meltingly sweet; the beef takes on the warm spicing without losing its meatiness.
sourceNotes: Stifado is the standard Greek slow-cooked stew, named for the technique (stifado meaning a long braise in red wine and vinegar with whole onions). The dish is made with beef most commonly today but is older with rabbit (kouneli stifado), hare (lagos stifado), or octopus (htapodi stifado), the cooking method holds across the proteins. The warm spice profile (cinnamon and cloves and allspice) is a marker of Ottoman influence on Greek cookery and dates the dish's modern shape to the late Ottoman period and the nineteenth century. Served traditionally with crusty bread, mashed potato, or rice. A small piece of feta on the plate is common.

content.text: A kilo of beef chuck cut into large cubes. Half a kilo of baby shallots, peeled but left whole. A bottle of red wine roughly half-used. Vinegar, tomato, cinnamon, cloves, allspice. Everything goes into a heavy casserole and cooks at 150°C for two and a half hours, until the beef is fork-soft and the shallots have collapsed inwards but kept their shape.

The peeling of the shallots is the only awkward task. Drop them into boiling water for thirty seconds first; the skins slip off with a fingernail. Trim the root flat but do not cut deep into it, or the layers separate in the pot. Keep them whole. The dish is named for the onions as much as the beef, and a stifado of pulped shallots is not stifado.

items.name: Beef chuck
items.prepNote: cut into 4 cm cubes, fat trimmed but not removed

items.name: Shallot
items.prepNote: small baby shallots, peeled and left whole

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: peeled and lightly crushed with the flat of a knife

content.text: Bring a kettle of water to a boil. Tip the {{shallot}} of unpeeled shallots into a bowl and pour the boiling water over them. Let them sit thirty seconds, drain, and slip the skins off with your fingernails. Trim the root flat but leave the layers attached. Pat the {{beef-chuck}} of beef cubes very dry on kitchen paper and season generously with salt and pepper. Wet meat does not brown.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the beef cubes in batches, about four minutes per batch, turning to colour all sides. Do not crowd; one layer at a time. Lift each browned batch onto a plate.

Lower the heat to medium. Add the peeled shallots to the casserole and cook for five minutes, turning occasionally, until they are golden on the outside. Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and cook for one minute more. Stir in the {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for one minute, until darkened. Pour in the {{red-wine}} of red wine and the {{red-wine-vinegar}} of vinegar; bring to a simmer and let bubble for two minutes.

Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tomatoes, the {{stock-beef}} of hot stock, the {{cinnamon-stick}} of cinnamon stick, the {{cloves}} of cloves, the {{allspice}} of allspice, and the {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves. Return the beef and any juices to the pot. Stir gently to settle the meat and shallots into the liquid.

Heat the oven to 150°C. Cover the casserole and transfer to the oven for two and a half hours. Check after ninety minutes; the liquid should be at a slow gentle simmer. If it is barely moving, raise the temperature by 10°C. Do not stir aggressively during the braise; the shallots break. After the full time, the beef should yield to a fork and the shallots should be tender right through. Lift the lid for the last twenty minutes if the sauce is thinner than you want.

Lift out the cinnamon stick and the spent bay. Taste the sauce; add salt or a splash more vinegar to lift if needed. Serve over mashed potato, with rice, or with a chunk of bread to drag through the sauce. A small piece of feta on the side balances the warm spice.

items.fix: Trim only the very base of the root, leaving the layers attached. Stir gently with a wooden spoon, just enough to redistribute.
items.cause: Cut too deep into the root, or stirred too vigorously during the braise.
items.symptom: Shallots have fallen apart into shreds

items.fix: Use chuck (braising steak), not lean cuts. The braise should hum at a slow simmer, not a fast one. Drop the oven by 10°C if the surface is bubbling actively.
items.cause: Wrong cut, or braise too hot.
items.symptom: Beef is dry and stringy at the end

items.fix: Boil the wine and vinegar for the full two minutes before the tomatoes and stock go in. The braise also mellows the acid; if it tastes sharp at the end, simmer uncovered for ten minutes more.
items.cause: Vinegar not reduced enough before adding the rest of the liquid.
items.symptom: Sauce is sharp from the vinegar

items.fix: Lift the cinnamon stick and pick out visible cloves at the two-hour mark. Whole spices keep releasing flavour until they are removed.
items.cause: Too much clove or allspice; or the cinnamon stick was left in too long.

items.fix: Uncover for the last twenty minutes. The sauce reduces and clings to the meat.
items.cause: Too much stock, or the lid was left tight on for the whole braise.

content.text: Rabbit stifado (kouneli stifado) uses jointed rabbit; reduce the braising time to ninety minutes. Octopus stifado (htapodi stifado) uses braised octopus and is a coastal-island dish from Cyprus and the Greek islands. For a lighter version, swap the beef for lamb shoulder cut into cubes; the cook time stays the same.

Stifado is the cold-weather Greek braise, made in winter when the kitchen wants to be warm for hours. It is the dish for Sunday lunch in the mountains, for a long table on a Saturday night, for the day after when leftovers feed a family on rice or bread. The warm spice profile is unusual in modern western cooking and is older than that; it points back to a time when cinnamon and cloves moved through the eastern Mediterranean by the cup, not the gram.
